title: VLMs (Vision-Language Models)
lesson:
A normal language model reads text and writes text. A Vision-Language Model (VLM) does the same, but it can also see. You hand it a photo, a screenshot, a chart, or a scanned receipt alongside your words, and it answers as if it had looked at the picture itself. "What's wrong with this chart?" "Read the total off this receipt." "Describe what's happening in this CCTV frame." In 2026 most flagship models you use every day (the big chat assistants, and open ones like Qwen-VL, Llama Vision, and Pixtral) are VLMs by default.

The core trick: turn pixels into tokens

You already know that a text model breaks words into Tokens (small chunks of text it processes one at a time). A VLM does the same thing to images. Here is the everyday analogy: imagine a translator who only speaks one language, Token. A photo arrives in a foreign language (pixels). Before the translator can discuss it, a helper must rewrite the photo into Token. That helper is the vision encoder a separate neural network (often a Vision Transformer, or ViT) that chops the image into a grid of small squares called patches, and turns each patch into a vector of numbers.

If "vector of numbers" sounds scary, it's the same idea as an Embeddings: a list of numbers that captures meaning, so two similar things sit close together. So a 512x512 photo might become, say, a few hundred image tokens. Those image tokens are then fed into the language model right next to your text tokens, as if they were just unusual words. The Attention Mechanism the part of a Transformers model that lets every token look at every other token then lets the words and the picture-patches talk to each other.

The glue in the middle

There's one catch. The vision encoder speaks its own dialect of numbers, and the language model expects a slightly different one. So a small bridge sits between them, usually called a projector or adapter (a tiny set of layers that reshapes the image vectors into the exact shape the language model wants). Second analogy: it's a power-plug adapter. The laptop (language model) is fine; the foreign socket (image encoder) is fine; you just need the little converter so they connect. Training a VLM often means freezing the two big parts and mostly training this cheap adapter so it lines the two worlds up.

So the full pipeline is: image -> vision encoder -> projector -> image tokens -> mixed with text tokens -> one transformer -> text out. The output is always text. A plain VLM reads images; it does not draw them. (Drawing pictures is a separate job done by image-generation models.)

What this is actually good for

  • Document and receipt reading pulling numbers, dates, and line items out of a scan, no brittle template needed.
  • Screenshots and UI the eyes behind Browser Agents, which look at a web page and decide where to click.
  • Charts and diagrams answering "which quarter dropped?" from a graph image.
  • Accessibility describing photos out loud for blind users.

Calling one looks almost like a normal chat call you just add an image part to the message:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-vision-2026",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": [
        {"type": "text", "text": "What is the total due?"},
        {"type": "image_url",
         "image_url": {"url": "data:image/png;base64," + img_b64}},
    ]}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)  # -> "The total due is $48.20"

Notice the image goes in as base64 (the picture file encoded as plain text characters) inside the same message as your question. The model handles the rest.

The thing that surprises everyone: images are expensive

Each image becomes hundreds or thousands of tokens, and those tokens fill your Context Windows (the model's limited short-term memory) and your bill. A single high-resolution screenshot can cost as much as a page of text. Most APIs let you pick a detail level: "low" downsizes the image to fewer tokens (cheap, fine for "is this a cat?"), while "high" keeps detail (needed to read tiny text). Pick the lowest level that still works.

💡 Rule of thumb: if you only need a yes/no or a category, send a small/low-detail image. Save high detail for when the model must read fine print.
mentalModel: A VLM is a text model wearing glasses: a vision encoder rewrites the picture into the same 'token' language the model already reads, so words and pixels sit side by side in one stream and the model answers in text.
mistakes: Expecting it to draw. A VLM reads images and outputs text. It cannot generate or edit a picture that's a separate image-generation model.
Ignoring the token cost. One high-res image can eat thousands of tokens. People send 4K screenshots and are shocked at the bill and the slow reply downsize first.
Sending tiny text at low detail. If the model 'can't read' the receipt, you probably downsized too far. For fine print, use high-detail mode or crop to the part that matters.
Assuming pixel-perfect coordinates. VLMs are great at what is in an image but often fuzzy on exact pixel positions and precise counting ('how many people?'). Verify before acting on coordinates.
Trusting OCR-style output blindly. The model can confidently misread a 3 as an 8 or hallucinate a value that isn't there. For money or legal data, validate against a checksum or a second pass.
Forgetting it still has a context limit. Image tokens count toward the window. Stuff in ten screenshots and your actual question may get squeezed out or truncated.
exercise:
Take a photo of a real receipt or a screenshot of a simple bar chart. Using any VLM you have access to (a hosted chat API, or a local one), send the image with the prompt: 'List every line item and its price as JSON, then give the total.' Compare its answer to the truth. Now resend the same image at a lower detail/resolution setting and note two things: did accuracy drop, and how did the token count (and cost) change?

Stretch goal: Run the same image three times at the same settings and check whether the extracted total is identical each time. If it wobbles, add a verification step (e.g. ask the model to re-add the line items and confirm they match the printed total) and report whether that catches the errors.
solution: You should see a clear trade-off: high detail reads small text reliably but uses far more tokens; low detail is cheap and fast but starts to misread fine print. For the stretch goal, totals often wobble slightly across runs because generation is probabilistic a self-check ('re-sum the items; do they equal the printed total?') catches many misreads. The lesson: treat VLM extraction as a draft that needs validation, not a trusted source, especially for numbers.
summary: A VLM is a language model that can also see. A vision encoder slices an image into patches and turns them into 'image tokens', a small projector lines them up with text tokens, and one transformer reasons over both producing text out. Great for documents, screenshots, and charts. Watch the token cost, choose the right detail level, and always verify extracted numbers.

keyTerms.def: A model that takes both images and text as input and produces text output letting you ask questions about a picture.
A separate network (often a Vision Transformer) that converts an image into vectors of numbers the language model can use.
A small square the image is chopped into (like grid tiles). Each patch becomes one image token.
An image patch turned into the same kind of numeric chunk a text token is, so words and pixels share one stream.
A small bridge of layers that reshapes the vision encoder's output into the exact form the language model expects; like a plug adapter.
An API setting (low/high) that controls how much of an image's resolution is kept low is cheaper, high is needed to read fine print.
Working with more than one kind of input (here, text plus images) in a single model.
Optical Character Recognition reading text out of an image. VLMs do this informally but can misread, so verify.
